# Concept Card: Services

## Definition
Transcript evidence থেকে derived canonical definition; conflicting views থাকলে সব variant রাখা হয়েছে।

## First Principles
System boundary, control loop, এবং failure behavior দৃষ্টিতে concept ব্যাখ্যা করতে হবে।

## Mental Model
এই concept-কে runtime contract হিসেবে ধরলে debugging ও design decisions সহজ হয়।

## Why It Exists
Operational complexity কমানো এবং reliable automation enable করা।

## Internal Working
Control plane state reconciliation + node-level execution pathway।

## YAML and Command Usage
Related raw commands এবং manifests reference-based রাখা হয়েছে; no rewrite of original evidence.

## Use Cases
Lab, production-like demos, and certification patterns থেকে mapped।

## Edge Cases
Version mismatch, policy conflict, scheduling/storage/network constraints উল্লেখ করা হয়েছে।

## Pitfalls
Warning/Pitfall entries evidence সহ retain করা হয়েছে।

## Troubleshooting Angle
Symptom -> Cause -> Fix logic chain evidence-based maintain করা হয়েছে।

## Exam Relevance
CKA/CKAD speed + correctness + troubleshooting expectations mapped।

## Source Contributions

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 033_Services.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When we create a service without us having to do any additional configuration, Kubernetes automatically creates a service that spans across all the nodes in the cluster and maps the target port to the same node port on all the nodes in the cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

### Evidence 157: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/033_Services.extraction.md::E114

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 033_Services.txt

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To summarize, in any case, whether it be a single pod on a single node, multiple pods on a single node, or multiple pods on multiple nodes, the service is created exactly the same without you having to do any additional steps during the service creation.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 328: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/038_Namespaces.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 038_Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When the cluster is first set up, Kubernetes creates a set of pods and services for its internal purpose, such as those required by the networking solution, the DNS service, etc. to isolate these from the user and to prevent you from accidentally deleting or modifying these services, Kubernetes creates them under another namespace created at cluster startup Dub named kube system.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Networking, Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 349: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E32
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In the Kubernetes world, the imperative way of managing infrastructure is using commands like the kubectl run command to create a pod, the kubectl create deployment command to create a deployment, the kubectl expose command to create a service to expose a deployment, and the kubectl edit command may be used to edit an existing object.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Configuration

### Evidence 350: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The declarative approach would be to create a set of files that defines the expected state of the applications and services on a Kubernetes cluster, and with a single kubectl apply command, Kubernetes should be able to read the configuration files and decide by itself what needs to be done to bring the infrastructure to the expected state.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1096: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/14_Troubleshooting/289_Control Plane Failure.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 14_Troubleshooting / 289_Control Plane Failure.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If we had control plane components deployed as pods in case of a cluster deployed with the kube ADM tool, then we can check to make sure that the pods in the kube system namespace are running, or else if the control plane components are deployed as services, as in our case, then check the status of the services such as the kube API server, the controller, manager and scheduler on the master nodes, and the Kubelet and kube proxy service on the worker nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Scheduling, Architecture, Troubleshooting

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1237: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/030_Services.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 030_Services.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: I want to be able to access the web server from my own laptop, without having to SSH into the node, and simply by accessing the IP of the Kubernetes node.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

### Evidence 1238: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/030_Services.extraction.md::E25

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 030_Services.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1241: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/030_Services.extraction.md::E28

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: One of its use case is to listen to a port on the node, and forward requests on that port to a port on the pod running the web application.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This type of service is known as a node port service, because the service listens to a port on the node and forward requests to the pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 1243: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/030_Services.extraction.md::E30

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1246: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/030_Services.extraction.md::E33

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The second is cluster IP and in this case the service creates a virtual IP inside the cluster to enable communication between different services, such as a set of front end servers to a set of back end backend servers.

### Evidence 1509: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: As you might already know, the user account is used by humans, and service accounts are used by machines.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1510: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A user account could be for an administrator accessing the cluster to perform administrative tasks, or a developer accessing the cluster to deploy applications, et cetera.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Architecture

### Evidence 1511: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A service account could be an account used by an application to interact with the Kubernetes cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Architecture

### Evidence 1512: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt

- Content: For example, a monitoring application like Prometheus uses a service account to pull the Kubernetes API for performance metrics.

- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt

### Evidence 1534: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E32
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You can create a service account, assign the write permissions using role-based access control mechanisms, and export your service account tokens, and use it to configure your third-party application to authenticate to the Kubernetes API.

### Evidence 1535: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E33
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But what if your third-party application is hosted on the Kubernetes cluster itself?
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Architecture

### Evidence 1536: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E34
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, we can have our custom Kubernetes dashboard application, or the Prometheus application deployed on the Kubernetes cluster itself.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Architecture, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1537: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E35
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In that case, this whole process of exporting the service account token and configuring the third-party application to use it can be made simple by automatically mounting the service token secret as a volume inside the pod hosting the third-party application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1538: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: That way, the token to access the Kubernetes API is already placed inside the pod and can be easily read by the application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1539: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Warning/Pitfall

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You don't have to provide it manually.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1540: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E38
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If you go back and look at the list of service accounts, you will see that there is a default service account that exists already.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1541: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E39
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For every namespace in Kubernetes, a service account named default is automatically created.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1542: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E40
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Each namespace has its own default service account.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1543: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E41
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Whenever a pod is created, the default service account and its token are automatically mounted to that pod as a volume mount.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1544: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E42
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, we have a simple pod definition file that creates a pod using my custom Kubernetes dashboard image.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1545: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E43
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We haven't specified any secrets or volume mounts in the definition file.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1546: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E44
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, when the pod is created, if you look at the details of the pod, by running the kubectl describe pod command, you see that a volume is automatically created from the secret named default token, which is in fact the secret containing the token for this default service account.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC, Configuration, On-Prem

### Evidence 1547: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E45

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The secret token is mounted at location var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount inside the pod.

### Evidence 1548: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E46
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So from inside the pod, if you run the ls command to list the contents of the directory, you will see the secret mounted as three separate files.

### Evidence 1549: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E47

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The one with the actual token is the file named token.

### Evidence 1550: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E48
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If you view contents of that file, you will see the token to be used for accessing the Kubernetes API Now remember that the default service account is very much restricted.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1551: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E49
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It only has permission to run basic Kubernetes API queries.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1552: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E50
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If you'd like to use a different service account such as the one we just created, modify the pod definition file to include a service account field and specify the name of the new service account.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1553: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E51
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Remember, you cannot edit the service account of an existing pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1554: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E52
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You must delete and recreate the pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1555: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E53
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, in case of a deployment, you will be able to edit the service account as any changes to the pod definition file will automatically trigger a new rollout for the deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1556: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E54
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the deployment will take care of deleting and recreating new pods with the right service account.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1557: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E55
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When you look at the pod details now, you see that the new service account is being used.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1558: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E56
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So remember, Kubernetes automatically mounts the default service account if you haven't explicitly specified any.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1559: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E57
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You may choose not to mount a service account automatically by setting the automountServiceAccountToken field to false in the pod spec section.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1560: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E58
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let's now discuss some of the changes that were made in releases version 1.22 and 1.24 of Kubernetes that changed the way service accounts secrets and tokens worked.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1561: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E59
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, as we discussed in the previous video, every namespace has a default service account, and that service account has a secret object with a token associated with it.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1562: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E60
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When a pod is created, it automatically associates the service account to the pod and mounts the token to a well-known location within the pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1563: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E61
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this case, it's under var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1564: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E62
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This makes the token accessible to a process that's running within the pod, and that enables that process to query the Kubernetes API.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, if you list the contents of the directory inside the pod, you will see the secret mounted as three separate files.

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The one with the actual token is the file named token.

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt

### Evidence 1568: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E66
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1569: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E67
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This is exactly what we discussed in the previous video.

### Evidence 1570: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E68
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now let's take that token that we just saw, and if you decode this token using this command, or you could just copy and paste this token in the JWT website at jwt.io, you'll see that it has no expiry date defined in the payload section here on the right.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1571: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E69
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So this is a token that does not have an expiry date set.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1572: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E70
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So this excerpt from the Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal for creating bound service account tokens describes this form of JWT to be having some security and scalability related issues.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Troubleshooting, On-Prem

### Evidence 1573: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E71
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the current implementation of JWT is not bound to any audience and is not time bound as we just saw.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1574: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E72
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There was no expiry date for the token.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1575: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E73
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the JWT is valid as long as the service account exists.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1576: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E74
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Moreover, each JWT requires a separate secret object per service account, which results in scalability issues.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 1577: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E75
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And as such, in version 1.22, the TokenRequestAPI was introduced as part of the Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal 1205 that aimed to introduce a mechanism for provisioning Kubernetes service account tokens that are more secure and scalable via an API.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1578: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E76
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So tokens generated by the token request API are audience bound, they're time bound and object bound, and hence are more secure.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1579: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E77
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, since version 1.22, when a new pod is created, it no longer relies on the service account secret token that we just saw.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1580: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E78
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Instead, a token with a defined lifetime is generated through the TokenRequestAPI by the service account admission controller when the pod is created.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1581: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E79
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And this token is then mount as a projected volume onto the pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1582: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E80
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So in the past, if you look at this space here, you'd see the secret that's part of the service account mount as a secret object.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1583: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E81
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But now as you can see, it's a projected volume that actually communicates with the token controller API, TokenRequestAPI, and it gets a token for the pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1584: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E82
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now with version 1.24, another enhancement was made as part of the Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal 2799, which dealt with the reduction of secret-based service account tokens.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1585: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E83
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So in the past, when a service account was created, it automatically created a secret with a token that has no expiry and is not bound to any audience.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1586: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E84
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This was then automatically mount as a volume to any pod that uses that service account.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1587: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E85
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And that's what we just saw.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1588: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E86
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But in version 1.22, that was changed.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1589: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E87
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The automatic mounting of the secret object to the pod was changed, and instead it then moved to the TokenRequestAPI.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1590: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E88
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So with version 1.24, a change was made where when you create a service account, it no longer automatically creates a secret or a token access secret.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1591: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E89
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So you must run the command kubectl create token, followed by the name of the service account to generate a token for that service account if you needed one.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1592: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E90
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1593: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E91
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, if you copy that token, and then if you try to decode this token, this time, you'll see that it has an expiry date defined.

### Evidence 1594: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E92

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1596: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E94
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So now post version 1.24, if you would still like to create secrets the old way with non-expiring token, then you could still do that by creating a secret object with the type set to kubernetes.io/service-account-token and the name of the service account specified within annotations in the metadata section like this.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt

### Evidence 1601: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E99
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, you have to be sure if you really wanna do that because as per the Kubernetes documentation pages on service account token secrets, it says you should only create service account token secrets if you can't use the TokenRequestAPI to obtain a token.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1602: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E100
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So that's either the kubectl create token command we just talked about, or it talks to the TokenRequestAPI to generate that token, or it's the automated token creation that happens on pods when they're created post version 1.22.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1603: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E101
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And also, you should only create service account token request if the security exposure of persisting and non-expiring token credential is acceptable to you.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

### Evidence 1604: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/065_Service Account.extraction.md::E102
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 065_Service Account.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, the TokenRequestAPI is recommended instead of using the service account token secret objects as they are more secure and have a bounded lifetime unlike the service account token secrets that have no expiry.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The idea of decoupling a large monolithic application into subcomponents known as microservices enable us to develop and deploy a set of independent, small and reusable code.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services

### Evidence 1677: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/04_Multi-Container Pods/080_Multi-Container Pods.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Architecture

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This architecture then helps us scale up and down, as well as modify each service as required, as opposed to modifying the entire application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 1678: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/04_Multi-Container Pods/080_Multi-Container Pods.extraction.md::E4

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, at times you may need two services to work together, such as a web server.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You don't want to merge and bloat the code of the two services, as each of them target different features and you still like them to be developed and deployed separately, you only need the two functionality to work together.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services

- Content: It could be a database service or a large web server serving front end users.

- Type: Implementation Step

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let's look at a simple scenario where you create a pod and expose it to external users using a service.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Observability

### Evidence 1685: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E41
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The service will route traffic to the pod immediately.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Observability

### Evidence 1686: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E42
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The service relies on the pod's ready condition to route traffic.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Observability

### Evidence 1687: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E45
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But if the application within the container took longer to get ready, the service is unaware of it and sends traffic through as a container is already in a ready state, causing users to hit a pod that isn't yet running a live application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Observability

### Evidence 1688: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E59
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Until then, the service does not forward any traffic to the pod as it sees that the pod is not ready.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Observability

### Evidence 1689: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E71
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Say you have a replica set or deployment with multiple pods and a service serving traffic to all the pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Observability

### Evidence 1690: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E73
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Say you were to add an additional pod and let's say the pod takes a minute to warm up, without the readiness probe configured correctly, the service would immediately start routing traffic to the new pod.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Observability

### Evidence 1691: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E74
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: That will result in service disruption to at least some of the users.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Observability

### Evidence 1692: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E75
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 086_Readiness and Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Instead, if the pods were configured with the correct readiness probe, the service will continue to serve traffic only to the older pods and wait until the new pod is ready.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Observability

### Evidence 1693: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/087_Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 087_Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Since Docker is not an orchestration engine, the container continues to stay dead and deny services to users until you manually create a new container.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Observability

### Evidence 1694: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/05_Observability/087_Liveness Probes.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 087_Liveness Probes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Every time the application crashes, Kubernetes makes an attempt to restart the container to restore service to users.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Observability

- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt

### Evidence 2928: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E111
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 2929: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E112
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And finally, let's look at what happens when the pods are distributed across multiple nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2930: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E113
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this case, we have the web application on pods on separate nodes in the cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2931: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E114
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When we create a service without us having to do any additional configuration, Kubernetes automatically creates a service that spans across all the nodes in the cluster and maps the target port to the same node port on all the nodes in the cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2932: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E115
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This way, you can access your application using the IP of any node in the cluster and using the same port number, which in this case is 30,008.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2933: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E116
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: As you can see, using the IP of any of these nodes, and I'm trying to curl to the same port and the same port is made available on all the nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2934: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E117
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Part of the cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2935: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E118
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To summarize, in any case, whether it be a single pod on a single node, multiple pods on a single node, or multiple pods on multiple nodes, the service is created exactly the same without you having to do any additional steps during the service creation.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2936: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E119
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When pods are removed or added, the service is automatically updated, making it highly flexible and adaptive.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2937: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E120
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this lecture we will discuss about the Kubernetes Service cluster IP.

### Evidence 2991: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/041_Services - ClusterIP.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 041_Services - ClusterIP.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A full stack web application typically has different kinds of pods hosting different parts of an application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2992: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/041_Services - ClusterIP.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 041_Services - ClusterIP.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You may have a number of pods running a front end web server, another set of pods running a back end server, a set of pods running a key value store like Redis, and another set of pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2993: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/041_Services - ClusterIP.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 041_Services - ClusterIP.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Maybe running a persistent database like MySQL.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

### Evidence 2994: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/041_Services - ClusterIP.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 041_Services - ClusterIP.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 041_Services - ClusterIP.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 041_Services - ClusterIP.txt

### Evidence 2997: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/041_Services - ClusterIP.extraction.md::E8

- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 3579: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It doesn't provide us a mechanism to easily scale up and scale down.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 3580: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: On top of that, if one of our applications goes down, there's nothing watching over it to restart it or deploy a new instance.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 3581: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Warning/Pitfall

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You don't get that functionality with just pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 3582: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And on top of that, there's no simple way to upgrade your application.
